source_title: #338 ‒ Peter's takeaways on aerobic exercise and VO2 max, insulin resistance, rising healthcare costs, treating children with autism and ADHD, and strength training | Quarterly Podcast Summary #4

paragraphs.speaker: peter
paragraphs.name: Peter Attia
paragraphs.text: Welcome to another special AMA episode. Today's the fourth installment of the quarterly podcast summary. In this conversation, I discuss what I learned from the last quarter of interviews and what I think were some of the most important insights as well as things that have resulted in a change in my work and behavior. Please note that listening to the quarterly podcast summary does not remotely constitute a substitute for listening to the actual episodes. My hope is that it helps you think about refining what you might've learned there.

paragraphs.speaker: peter
paragraphs.name: Peter Attia
paragraphs.text: We cover the interviews I did with Olav, Alexander Boo, Ralph DeFranco, Sam Sutaria, Trenna Sutcliffe, and Mike Estretel. Throughout these, we talk on various topics: VO2 max, power at VO2 max, insulin resistance, metformin, SGLT2 inhibitors, GLP-1 agonists, US healthcare costs, diagnosis and treatment approaches to autism, ADHD and anxiety, zone 2 resistance training, and learning all of the effects of anabolic steroids.

paragraphs.speaker: peter
paragraphs.name: Peter Attia
paragraphs.text: This might end up being the longest quarterly summary because by the nature of coincidence and which podcasts fall into the past quarter, this is probably the most voluminous set of notes I've ever taken. Two of the episodes we've covered here, the one with Ralph and the one with Sam, were easily three to four times the volume of notes I normally take.

paragraphs.speaker: peter
paragraphs.name: Peter Attia
paragraphs.text: We started the Olav discussion by explaining the difference between functional threshold power and critical power. The functional threshold power or FTP is the power that you can hold for one hour. Much more typically, when I was a cyclist, we would do this in a 20 minute test and we would discount it by typically 10%, although Olav suggested only 5%. There's something called critical power, which is very similar, but rather than it being the power you can hold for an hour, it's the power you can hold for 30 to 40 minutes. Critical power is much easier to measure. You can do it from a set of curves that are derived from three to four individual tests that are much shorter.

paragraphs.speaker: peter
paragraphs.name: Peter Attia
paragraphs.text: A very interesting insight that came up was talking about the relationship between VO2 max and PVO2 max. PVO2 max means what is your power when you are at VO2 max. Some have argued that VVO2 max or PVO2 max are actually more predictive of sports specific performance than just the number VO2 max. I shared my numbers with Olav and the truth is I have always had a very low PVO2 max to VO2 max. I have always had a VO2 max that is higher than many people who are much better cyclists than me. What Olav suggested is it speaks to really inefficient, probably overtraining aerobically, undertraining anaerobically. There might be an association between people like that and a lesser propensity to gain weight. Every time I've done a resting metabolic expenditure test or the doubly-labeled water test in the metabolic chambers, I always seem to have a through-the-roof energy expenditure for my body weight. I have an advantage in that I have a very high energy expenditure, but the flip side is I'm actually quite inefficient at utilizing energy.

paragraphs.speaker: peter
paragraphs.name: Peter Attia
paragraphs.text: The single most important takeaway from this podcast for high-performance athletes is what the upper limits of carbohydrate consumption are while doing cardio activity and racing. Conventional thinking has always been that you can only consume about 60 grams of glucose per hour and 60 grams of glucose is only about 240 kilocalories. This has always been one of my main arguments for why being fat adapted is very important. What we've seen unquestionably is the biggest innovation in endurance sports like the Tour de France and Ironman over the past decade is the amount of glucose that these guys are able to consume. Olav said that they're now pushing triathletes at the world class level to 240 grams per hour. You can meet all of your glycolytic needs indefinitely through that. These are athletes that are required to now consume gels and eat carbohydrates at a 12% mixture. What does that mean? A 12% mixture means 120 grams per liter. Conventional wisdom is that we can't tolerate more than a 5% mixture. What Olav said and what Tadej has also said separately is you can actually train your GI system to double that. When you look at the hyperbolic performance of endurance athletes today, another explanation is that they literally figured out how to double the octane of the fuel.
